posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked: In Europe, owning your own vineyards for centuries, having very large volume, and having your premises and equipment paid off helps as does only aging the wine for 12-18 months. In the US, having European ownership helps. People can knock the basic Segura Viudas Brut Reserva wine, but it isn’t a bad wine, just not interesting, but how, for many years, Costco could sell magnums of it for $9-$10 is beyond me.

posts.name: Alex Nichols
posts.cooked:
Could someone post the full notes/release letter - just want to get a better sense of everything… Thanks!

Making still wines is a slow process compared to say, building a chair. Generally there is feedback along the way, and after fermentation the surprises are, hopefully, over. Making sparkling wine on the other hand has a different feel. There’s still the first part of harvest and élevage, but then comes the black box of secondary fermentation. Locked away in a tirage bin, sur lies, for 40+ months, the resultant wine has a homecoming of sorts. Disgorging a vintage for the first time can surprise, and the wines of 2016 did that for us. They are excellent.

The 2016 vintage was our 7th with Charlie Heintz. It was an extremely early vintage, with the Pinot Noir being picked on the 15th of August and the Chardonnay soon after, on the 2nd of September. For reference, the extremely early 2020 vintage had us picking Pinot Noir and Chardonnay in August – a first. Often we confuse earliness with late season heat, riper fruit, and lower acidity, but that is absolutely not the case in 2016. The wines are linear and powerful and represent a refinement in the style of Ultramarine. The Blanc de noirs is one of my favorite wines to date. It’s pure, smoky and savory, with a hint of berry jam and fruitiness on the nose, and an extremely long finish. The Blanc de blancs has its signature aromas of pâtisserie and lemon curd that is always present. Surprisingly the wine is light and almost playful while still retaining its salty, mineral finish. The Rosé is a pale pink with a strong character of strawberry cream and an intense acidity that adds structure and body. It is an alluring wine.

These wines are definitely on the more serious side and represent many years of learning and perfecting of our technique, both in the vineyard and at the winery. Those who wait six months to a year post disgorgement will be greatly rewarded. On the other hand, those looking for something to toast to good health in 2021 should look to the Blanc de blancs first and know that we’ll be doing the same.

Finally, we are offering a few bottles of the late disgorged 2012. These wines are not guaranteed, but sold on a first come, first-served basis. We have about a hundred bottles of both the Blanc de blancs and the Blanc de noirs that we have left on lees in bottle for a total of 7 years. The acidity in both has mellowed and integrated. The wines overall are significantly richer than the normal disgorgements. We do think of these wines as experiments and not a “better” Ultramarine. Enjoy them with that in mind and we’d love to hear your feedback.

Wishing you all the best of health. Thank you so much for your continued support and help in making Ultramarine the wine that it is today.

Cheers,
Michael and the rest of the Ultramarine crew.

PURCHASE
ALLOCATIONS
Wines are available only in a 3-pack. Your allocation for the 2016 Selection is guaranteed for two days and then will be sold on a first-come, first-served basis to the rest of our mailing list.
*The 2012 vintage wines are not sold as part of your guaranteed allocation.
*Any wish list requests for the 2016 Rosé will be granted on September 18th if available, and you will be notified via email.

SHIPPING DATES
We will be shipping via FedEx the first week of December, weather permitting, and will contact you prior to shipping. We are sorry, but there is no pick up option this release.

posts.name: Tim Williams
posts.cooked:
Question in general, why do wineries with long waiting lists such as UM allow active customers to wish list vs. allowing some on the wait list to buy for the first time? Is it so they don’t over extend their active buyer lists for future years in which production is lower? Just curious as I’m obviously a disgruntled wait lister jealous of all you buyers… (Kidding on disgruntled, but curious anyhow)

Because good business is to sell 100% of your goods to the lowest number of buyers possible. Less transactions. Less logistics. More exclusivity to keep demand high. If they didn’t sell through 100% I’m sure it would be a different story.

If you are ever around PHL we can pop a cork.

TW

Question in general, why do wineries with long waiting lists such as UM allow active customers to wish list vs. allowing some on the wait list to buy for the first time? Is it so they don’t over extend their active buyer lists for future years in which production is lower? Just curious as I’m obviously a disgruntled wait lister jealous of all you buyers… (Kidding on disgruntled, but curious anyhow)

I would come from it at the idea that if someone is on the list they’re going to stay on the list. I worked for Saks corp. in the late 00s and there was the “surprise & delight” motto that went around the store & the buying offices. I WL’d for 1 bottle of the Keefer but didn’t buy 1 bottle of the Rosé that I could have. I am delighted that I get that single bottle of wine, I was surprised when I was offered 2 allocations of the 3 pack which I grabbed. As someone else mentioned it is easier to keep/retain an existing customer - shipping to 500 people instead of 1500 is a lot less time & resources (just think about how many people require a 3 pack etc because that’s easier than shipping 1 bottle to 3 people).
He could probably make the allocation for a mixed case that you have to pick up yourself and still sell out.

From the mailer:

And finally, we have a new vineyard to announce. Our first non-Heintz release is from Keefer Ranch. Only a few miles as the crow flies from Charlie Heintz’s vineyard, but a hundred miles away in flavor profile. The nose is more classically Chardonnay, green apple with the slightest hint of caramel. The palate is bright and mineral. More wet stone than saline, the acid though high, has a smoother, softer texture. It is dosed at 2 g/L.

posts.name: Fred Daniels
posts.cooked:
Interesting comment on “good Champagne”…you’re right…if you put this up against Cristal and Dom and Clos de Goisses you’ll wonder what the fuss is about. But I think that the terroir in Champagne is so specialized and the techniques so engrained that quality is high and most NV blends would blow away 95% of the rest of the world’s sparkling wines.

I had a Ultramarine '14 Bl de Noirs yesterday (no Champagne to be found) and it was special. Nailed the fine, persistent pinpoint mousse, rich but balanced flavors, some complexity and incredible balance that made the wine float across the palate. So I’d say aging well, and more to come. It may not be Champagne, but it might be the best sparkling wine made in California…so if I’m drinking local, this is at the top of the heap. I.e., if I’m doing a top of the line California menu, with say Dominus and Maya and Marcassin…one of the Ultramarines would be my sparkling choice.

As to other great sparkling wines from CA, I’m open to suggestions but that is another thread.

posts.name: Steve L Gellman
posts.cooked:
Received this email from Flatiron, back vintages of ultramarine available. No idea if prices are reasonable

Dear Friends,

In a refreshing change from our usual private cellar offers of Burgundy and Barolo, I’m pleased to offer today back vintages primarily from just two producers: Ultramarine and Williams Selyem.

Ultramarine is a true sensation. It embodies the very best of Champagne’s grower movement, with its focus on single vineyards, single varieties and sinigle vintages, minimal dosage, and traditional wine-making. Except that it is not Champagne, but Californian sparkling wine! The wines have become very hard to source, and we are pleased to offer this collection with vintages that go back to Ultramarine’s beginnings.

Williams Selyem is a well-known producer of elegant-styled Pinot Noir from cooler Californian sites. They have a track record of aging beautifully, and many of the wines being offered today are now entering their peak window.

Both sets of wines were acquired from local collectors who were lucky enough to be on the producers’ mailing lists, and who stored the wines witih care. Bottle conditions look great. A few wines from other producers round out the collection.

Please click here to see what’s available and to purchase wine.

Thank you,
Jeff

(subject to our usual terms and conditions, including limited availability, sales taxes and shipping policies)

I’ve bought since 2012 and yes allocation was down to two three-packs. Maybe there was not enough Bl de Bl to go around? Of course with the premium and tax and shipping the actual cost is 30% more but you don’t see that on “wine searcher”. All they see is that there is plenty available at seemingly lower prices. While Flatiron has a good mailing list and could sell out the wines at $229 and over, Winebid will continue to drag on the aftermarket. It’s even hard for K&L to sell a bottle over $200 at auction…they do much better just selling it at retail outright.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
I shared an allocation with a friend and lurker on here for years and have been reporting a steady increase in weirdly flavored bottles across multiple vintages.

2017 Ultramarine Blanc de Noirs Charles Heintz Vineyard - This wine is showing elevated acids and minerality that somewhat hides a nice fruit profile, which is bright with lemon, musky white cherry, and a hint of peach. The issue is the aggressive dill or grassy weedy note that turns the palate sour. It is overwhelming, to me, and does not blow off with air. Almost reminds me of a very green note in a Bachelet, and one must fight hard to distinguish the enjoyable elements below the sour dill smell and taste. This wine becomes work with time. I was so confounded by this wine that I went to cellar tracker where a taster noted a yellow onion aroma. Mind you, that taster also somehow managed to give this wine 92 or 93 points despite onion in his champagne. I am not that generous. While it has bright acidity and good minerality, a fine mousse, and good texture, the funky sour note seems a very material flaw. I would rate this Flawed, NR or, if feeling vicious, somewhere in the mid 80s. If I owned more of these, I would sell them immediately or offer to trade them for just about anything with bubbles.

2017 Ultramarine Heinz Blanc de Blancs I just don’t really get it, folks. Prior iterations of these Ultramarine wines have been lush and enjoyable, even if needing perhaps a bit more verve. I’ve really not enjoyed these 2017s and wish I’d sold them all. I’m seeing crazy high scores on CT (disregard any note with “brioche,” trust me) and I don’t understand. The nose is dominated by kafir lime leaf, lime oil, green apple jolly rancher, and, if I’m being super generous, maybe a soft floral note and some kiwi. Acids are higher here than past vintages, but to me the wine and flavor profile is a big miss, with an almost green note and dominant kafir lime leaf distracting me from any real enjoyment. This would be a score in the 80s for me, which will move the needle zero, as these Ultramarines are pure gold to the fanboy club. And yes, I bought a full allocation + extended tirage offering for the next year. A glutton for punishment, I guess.

2018 Ultramarine Hirsch Vineyard Rose - I slammed the 18 Heintz rose from Ultramarine about six months ago for being a syrupy sarsaparilla and funky cherry cola driven Colin wannabe mess that, to me, came across medicinal. This is very different but also disappointing. Instead of medicinal it is just a bit boring and savory. It opens with a bit of strawberry and raspberry, which quickly turns to thyme and savory herbs, a hint of blood orange, and maybe a touch of spice. It is just not all that complex, not all that smooth and integrated, and not all that interesting. It’s an okay rose, just not a good rose. And here it lacks the forward crowd pleasing nature of the Drappier and the intellectual depth of a truly fine wine. 89-90sh range for me.

From Champagne Thread poster J_Rock

Not Champagne, but we opened a 2019 Ultramarine Blanc de Noirs Heintz Vineyard and, as agreed by all 6 people who tasted it, the most prominent note was… asparagus. Otherwise, the wine seemed sound. Has anyone encounter this note on sparkling or white wine? Is this the sign of a specific flaw you can think of? I’m going to email Michael Cruse to see what he thinks, but wants to check here first.

posts.name: Jeff "Ozzie" Sattler
posts.cooked:
Very interesting thread. I thought I was missing something with UM because their 2017 and 2018 BdB and BdN were just not that appealing. Very dry and sharp which I thought was due to my dislike for that style in Sparklers/Champagne. Now I’m seeing a lot of distrust about UM in general with bottle variation and off-putting flavors. Too bad.

Also surprised to hear comments about Michael’s lack of response to questions about quality. Reminds me of what is said about Helen Turley at Marcassin and their, apparent, lack of concern for customers (I did receive a cryptic offer for the 2013 Marcassin Pinot last year, but just too many hoops to jump through to “maybe” have my request granted so didn’t even try). There’s just too much wine out there from which to choose to not respect your customers. (shout out to those who do: Roy Piper, Alex MACDONALD, the Realm folks, Shae Kinsman, among others)

I will say about UM that Mrs. Ozziewine and I love their Rose’s. Rose’s in general (not UMs) can be a tad cloying but UMs dry style, for us, seems to really complement their Rose’ genre. Same for the Cruse Tradition Rose’. Stellar.

I too dropped off the UM list last year as the grower Champagne appeals more (along with being reasonably priced) at this point in time: Jean Vesselle, Michel Dervin, Vilmart & Cie (pricier) and Michel Turgy to name just a few.

posts.cooked:
Ozzie_S:

In general they can be a tad cloying but UMs dry style, for us, seems to really complement their Rose’ genre

posts.name: Jeff "Ozzie" Sattler
posts.cooked:
Sure. For us, Rose’s (not Ultramarines’) are sometimes a little too sweet or rich, cloying to us. Whereas we find Ultramarines’, and the Cruse Tradition Rose as well, a more dry version of Rose while still maintaining that nice strawberry essence with complexity and without being cloying.

I can see that when I say the BdB and BdN are too dry I’m sending a mixed message when I say we like their Rose’ because it’s dry. I guess we like a Brut with a touch of sweetness and Rose’s that are drier.

You’re right though, cloying is not a hallmark of dry. For sure just attribute our feeling about the UM to personal preference.

And this is more of a “heads up” to those interested in trying them as opposed to a knock on their appeal, because, clearly their fan base is huge. And kudos to them for building it!

posts.cooked: I mean, it’s hard to say without tasting the same bottle, and I’ve only ever had very good experiences with Ultramarine (I finally got an allocation this year and am quite excited about it). A priori, a sort of asparagus-like aroma can indeed come from a combination of marginal/arrested maturity (i.e. ripeness) plus reduction; but trying to extrapolate from others’ tastings notes to what they were tasting is at best a fools’ errand, especially when the issue could be a defective bottle/cork rather than any kind of systematic one.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
J_Dornellas:

I guess that would be my bigger question, can those flavors and aromas come from many different causes or is it a narrow range.

As @William_Kelley mentioned, without having said bottles in front of yourself, it’s impossible to say the cause itself.

That said, one of the faults that seems to be consistent is a strong green aroma, oftentimes described like asparagus. This certainly could be due to unripe fruit - but I’ve personally never experienced that fault in sparkling. Therefore, it makes me believe there is another fault at play.

Cheers
